cataloging_subject.label: Saint Antony: Life, Finding, and Translation
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the three-part Middle English prose compilation transmitted continuously in British Library Royal MS 17 C XVII, fols. 124v-133r, and edited by Karl Horstmann on printed pp. 116-138. Its parts are a partial life of Antony the Great from the Athanasius-Evagrius tradition (BHL 609), a legendary finding and first translation of his relics to Constantinople (BHL 612), and a second translation into the region of Vienne (BHL 613). CME00090 encodes the complete Middle English edition but excludes Horstmann's introduction and dialect study on pp. 109-115. It is not Horstmann's complete article, the entire manuscript, or one undifferentiated saint's life.

titles.value: Saint Antony: Life, Finding, and Translation

assertion.notes: A concise English title distinguishing the three component genres and Antony the Great from Anthony of Padua.

notes: Horstmann's heading within the serial article Prosalegenden V.

titles.value: Here begyns þe fyndyng of þe glorios confessour Antone, þe gret ermyt

scope.description: Opening rubric as printed by Horstmann; he argues that it properly belongs to the second component rather than the complete compilation.

titles.value: Here begynnys þe fyndyng of þe gloryus confessour Antony & grete hermete
Here begynnys þe translacyone of þe gloryus confessor and hermet saynt Antony of Vienensis

scope.description: One or more unidentified translators of the three Latin components into Middle English

assertion.notes: No source establishes that one person translated all three parts or identifies the exact Latin exemplar.

Horstmann infers from the same hand and colophon that the scribe named elsewhere as Johannes Presbyter/Purvare may have compiled the unit. The current BL catalog does not make that responsibility certain.

assertion.notes: The witness is dated 1400-1440. It supplies a terminus ante quem for the surviving Middle English versions, not a precise translation date.

Witness/scribal language, not a proved place of composition

assertion.notes: The current BL catalog labels the codex's English 'Northern (?)'.

scope.description: Horstmann's tentative linguistic localization of the witness

assertion.notes: Horstmann describes mixed Midland inflections and mostly Northern vocalism but tentatively places the dialect in the western group, probably western Middle England.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Brief Latin incipits, biblical quotations, and the colophon are embedded in a substantively Middle English prose work and do not make the whole-work language facet multilingual.

form_statements.description: All three medieval components are prose; the XML contains no L or LG verse elements.

subject_statements.label: Saint Anthony's fire and its relief

content_parts.label: I. Life of Saint Antony
content_parts.summary: A Middle English translation of the opening portion of the Athanasian life in Evagrius's Latin tradition: Antony renounces wealth, pursues ascetic and solitary discipline, withstands sexual and demonic temptations, and emerges physically and spiritually unbroken after twenty years in seclusion.
notes: BHL 609; the BL catalog identifies the translated span as Evagrius's Latin version of Athanasius, chapters 1-13.
The Middle English manuscript rubric's claim that Jerome translated the source from Greek to Latin conflicts with the current catalog and BHL attribution.

content_parts.label: II. Finding and first translation of Saint Antony's relics
content_parts.summary: A legendary account in which the emperor's demon-possessed daughter Sophia can be cured only through Antony's body. Bishop Theophilus and twelve clerics find the hidden relics in Egypt and bring them through a sequence of miracles to Constantinople, where Sophia is delivered and the body enshrined.
notes: BHL 612. The BL catalog describes the component as the first translation to Constantinople and identifies the Latin account printed in Analecta Bollandiana 2 (1883), pp. 341-354.
Printed p. 122 is shared with the end of part I.

content_parts.label: III. Translation of Saint Antony's relics into the Vienne region
content_parts.summary: A Middle English version of the relic-translation narrative connecting Antony's body with Vienne: Jocelin receives the relics at Constantinople, a descendant entrusts them to monks of Montmajour, a monastery and hospital are founded, and Antony's aid is invoked against the burning illness called Saint Anthony's fire.
notes: BHL 613. The next codex component, medical recipes, begins on fol. 133v.
Printed p. 135 is shared with the end of part II.

summaries.value: A three-part Middle English prose compilation about Antony the Great. It moves from an abridged account of his ascetic life and demonic trials to legendary narratives of the discovery of his body, its miraculous journey to Constantinople, and its later translation into France, where his relics heal sufferers from the burning disease associated with his name. The anonymous English versions draw on three distinct Latin hagiographic texts cataloged as BHL 609, 612, and 613.
Part I: life and ascetic formation. Part II: finding and first translation of the relics to Constantinople. Part III: second translation into the Vienne region and cult of Saint Anthony's fire.

related_works.title: Vita Antonii, Latin translation by Evagrius (BHL 609)
notes: Part I translates only the opening portion; the exact physical Latin exemplar is unidentified.

related_works.title: Inventio and first translation of Saint Antony (BHL 612)
notes: Part II's Latin source work; the attribution to Theophilus belongs to the legendary textual frame and is not treated as confirmed historical authorship.

related_works.title: Second translation of Saint Antony into Gaul (BHL 613)
notes: Part III's Latin source work; the exact physical exemplar is unidentified.

extent.description: One DIV1 compilation containing three DIV2 parts, 23 PB elements, 140 NOTE elements, 189 MILESTONE elements, 2,161 HI elements, and 13 P elements. The encoded medieval text covers Horstmann's printed pp. 116-138; no verse L or LG elements occur.

notes: Antony is Antony the Great, the Egyptian abbot and hermit, not Anthony of Padua.
The BL catalog's 166-leaf extent supersedes Horstmann's statement that the manuscript then contained 162 leaves; both belong to their dated physical-description layers.
Horstmann's dialect localization and the current catalog's Northern (?) label are preserved as competing witness-language assessments and are not converted into a place of composition.
The many italic HI spans in CME00090 reproduce Horstmann's editorial typography, including supplied or expanded letters and German notes; they are not direct evidence of manuscript emphasis.
The complete Anglia volume, BHL reference volume, Analecta Bollandiana volume, and Acta Sanctorum volume are cached locally. No complete public facsimile of Royal MS 17 C XVII was verified.

open_questions.question: Did one Middle English translator or compiler assemble all three components, and was that person the Johannes Presbyter/Purvare named elsewhere in Royal MS 17 C XVII?
next_steps: Consult a modern codicological and paleographical study of Royal MS 17 C XVII.
Compare translation technique and vocabulary across all three parts.
Retain Johannes only as Horstmann's tentative compiler inference until stronger evidence appears.

open_questions.question: How should the manuscript's dialect be localized in current Middle English dialectology?
next_steps: Check LALME/eLALME and later linguistic studies for the manuscript's linguistic profile.
Reconcile the current catalog's Northern (?) label with Horstmann's mixed Midland/western proposal.
Keep scribal language separate from composition and translation place.

open_questions.question: Can the British Library supply a complete color digital facsimile of Royal MS 17 C XVII, including covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, and every manuscript leaf?
next_steps: Ask BL Reference Services about complete whole-object imaging and costs, quoting Royal MS 17 C XVII.
Map the target work separately to fols. 124v-133r in any supplied complete object.
Do not infer publication or repository redistribution permission for supplied images without written terms.

agents.name: Anonymous Middle English translator or translators
notes: The collective label does not assert one person or collaboration across all three parts.

agents.name: Johannes Purvare (?)
notes: Named in colophons elsewhere in Royal MS 17 C XVII; Horstmann tentatively identifies him as the compilation's scribe-compiler.

evidence.locator: component 20; physical description; access; digitised content

evidence.citation: Socii Bollandiani, Bibliotheca Hagiographica Latina Antiquae et Mediae Aetatis, vol. 1 (Brussels, 1898), entries 609, 612, 613.
evidence.locator: Saint Antony entries, printed pp. 99-100 / PDF pp. 140-141
evidence.summary: Standard hagiographic reference identifying the Athanasius-Evagrius life, first translation, and translation into Gaul as distinct Latin works.

evidence.citation: 'De S. Antonio Abbate: Incipit translatio sancti Anthonii confessoris,' Analecta Bollandiana 2 (1883), 341-354.
evidence.summary: Latin text corresponding to BHL 612 and Middle English part II, from the opening under Constantius and Theophilus through the discovery, journey, exorcism and Constantinople enshrinement.

evidence.citation: Acta Sanctorum, Januarii II, 3rd edition (Paris and Rome, 1863), 515-516, 'Eadem historia ex veteri MS. Ultrajectino.'
evidence.summary: Latin text and commentary corresponding to BHL 613 and Middle English part III, including the Vienne translation, Montmajour connection and cult against sacer ignis.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English item CME00090 and collection.
evidence.summary: Official stable identifier and digital-item context for CME00090; the current environment encountered a Cloudflare challenge on direct item retrieval, so structural claims are grounded in the checked-in XML and inspected edition.
